clean_story:
Distance over time. Motion measured.

The word speed comes from the Old English spēd, meaning "success" or "quick movement"—because moving faster often means achieving more in less time.

Speed isn’t just "how fast something goes." It’s the exact ratio: distance traveled divided by time taken. A cheetah sprinting at 120 kilometers per hour covers a football field in 3 seconds. A snail’s top speed? 0.05 kilometers per hour—slower than a minute hand on a clock. Both are precise; neither is "wrong."

Humans chase speed to shrink delays. A 19th-century letter crossed the Atlantic in weeks by ship; today, an email flies in milliseconds via undersea cables. Trains in Japan glide at 320 kilometers per hour, their wheels never touching the track—magnets lift them, cutting friction like a knife through air. Yet even "instant" has limits: light, the universe’s speed champion, takes 8 minutes to reach Earth from the Sun.

Speed demands trade-offs. A peregrine falcon dives at 390 kilometers per hour but risks blacking out from the G-forces. Supersonic jets break the sound barrier, yet their sonic booms shake windows 40 kilometers away. Every gain in speed reveals new constraints—heat, energy, control.

Three related subjects are friction’s hidden costs, time dilation in physics, and how animals outrun predators.
